data.title: PDO: This Nearly 11% Yielder Is Not Just For Income Investors
data.description:
Interest Rate Environment Led to a Great 2025

I last covered PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) in March 2025. I rated it a Hold at that time, as the trend of beneficial yield curve steepening seemed to be slowing, and PIMCO was reducing leverage and risk across its taxable closed-end funds. The conclusion in that article was,

I would wait for the fund to trade at a discount or for signs of yield curve steepening to resume before buying more.

The buying opportunity emerged shortly after that when the April 2025 "Liberation Day" Tariff Tantrum knocked the share price down below net asset value.

The 3-month - 10-year yield curve, a proxy for the difference between investment returns and the cost of leverage, also began steepening again, especially towards the end of 2025 as the Federal Reserve resumed cutting the Fed Funds rate.

In the steepening yield curve environment, PDO benefitted by remaining more leveraged than most of the other PIMCO taxable CEFs as the cost of leverage declined. The fund also benefitted from its relatively high exposure to mortgage-backed securities as MBS spreads narrowed from unusually wide levels in 2024. This contributed to PDO having the second-best total return of all PIMCO taxable CEFs over the past year at 14.39%, a nearly equity-like return for this predominantly fixed income fund.

Looking forward, PDO should continue to benefit from short-term rate cuts, allowing the cost of leverage to decline further. The fund remains heavily exposed to MBS, which may not experience the price appreciation of 2025 but now earn higher income after a few years of higher mortgage rates. PDO appears to be diversifying, with nearly 15% of the fund NAV invested in equities. The fund is also well-prepared to take advantage of downturns with nearly 10% in cash and cash equivalents as of year-end 2025.

PDO is not just for income investors, however. As we saw in 2025, the fund can have equity-like returns suitable for total return investors. Those who reinvest the dividends are helped by PIMCO's DRIP, which allows the monthly distributions to be reinvested at up to a 5% discount to market price. This can result in even higher total returns than shown on the charts above.

While PDO got off to a bad start at its IPO in 2021 thanks to an increasing interest rate environment, the fund has experienced a more favorable environment in the past year, earning a 5-star rating from Morningstar. With further declines in the cost of leverage, stable long-term rates, and continued low default rates in 2026, PDO can have another strong year.

Reduced Cost of Leverage

With $1.86 billion of net asset value, PDO is the third largest of the PIMCO taxable CEFs. That NAV is comprised of $2.87 billion of investments, offset by $1 billion of borrowings for a leverage ratio of 35.1%. That is the second-highest leverage of the group.

PDO borrows predominantly through the use of reverse repurchase agreements plus a small amount of credit default swaps. In a reverse repo, the bank buys securities from the borrower with the intention to sell them back at a slightly higher price later. In essence, it is a collateralized loan. At year-end 2025. PDO had 139 reverse repos on the books at an average interest rate of 4.2% and maturities ranging from January to June 2026. Reverse repo rates tend to move with short-term interest rates, which is why we see that PDO's expense ratio was 5.22% on 6/30/2025, down from 6.63% on 6/30/2024. This was an even bigger drop than the 1.0% worth of Fed Funds rate cuts in that time period. Since then, there have been 3 more Fed Funds rate cuts, or 0.75% total. The CME FedWatch Tool predicts another 1-2 cuts in 2026. Each 1% cut in the cost of leverage reduces PDO expenses by $10 million per year, or around $0.072 per share, enough to improve the coverage of each monthly distribution by about 5%.

Fund Holdings

On a duration-weighted equivalent basis, PDO is heavily concentrated in mortgage-backed securities, predominantly non-agency MBS. This is similar to two other PIMCO Funds, PDI and PAXS. Two other funds, PGP and RCS, have more conservative portfolios and invest primarily in agency MBS.

MBS spreads narrowed in 2025 for reasons I noted in March: Interest rate volatility is down (lowering the value of the prepayment option effectively given to borrowers). Also, many banks are back in the market to buy MBS, and the lack of banking crises caused fewer banks to have to sell MBS. For 2026, there is the additional catalyst of the Trump administration's request to Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to buy $200 billion worth of MBS. A "Goldilocks" environment for long-term interest rates would also be a positive for MBS values this year. Rates need to be high enough to earn decent interest income but not so high that they encourage prepayment. The 10-year Treasury note yield (US10Y) has cooperated, trading in the narrow range of 4.0-4.5% over the past year.

While PDO is still highly concentrated in MBS, they have trimmed this exposure about 11% since my March 2025 article. Replacing this was an increased exposure to foreign bonds, both developed (+2%) and emerging (+4%). PDO was the only fund in the group to significantly increase EM exposure in the past year. The emerging category contains government bonds of Turkey, Romania, Colombia, Ecuador, and Argentina, among others. There are even still bonds from Russia and Ukraine in the portfolio, marked well below par, so there is capital gain potential in the event of a peace deal. PDO (as well as PAXS, PDI, and PDX) increased investment-grade domestic bond exposure in the past year. In the case of PDO, it was up 5%. A significant new addition was the bonds of Beignet, a joint venture between Meta (META) and Blue Owl (OWL) to build a data center in Louisiana. These bonds make up 2% of the fund's NAV. I discussed this investment more in my recent article on PDX.

PDO now has about 15% of NAV invested in equity, including common and preferred stocks as well as mortgage REITs. Many of the stock positions are from bankruptcy and other reorganizations, including supply chain manager Incora and Italian bank Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMDPF). These workouts can produce capital gains for a patient holder over time. An example of this is AmSurg, which is set to be acquired by private healthcare system Ascension.

Not all the equity positions are from reorganizations. Some are from new ventures, such as a 2.9% NAV position in Mustang Express preferred shares. Mustang Express is a natural gas pipeline connecting storage hubs around Houston with LNG export facilities in Port Arthur, TX.

Finally, PDO maintains a significant cash position, with about 1.3% of NAV in Treasury Bills and 8.5% in PIMCO's internal short-term cash fund. This should provide a significant amount of buying power when opportunities arise.

Get More Total Return With The DRIP

PIMCO closed-end funds, including PDO, offer a dividend reinvestment plan that allows shareholders to reinvest their dividends at up to a 5% discount. This plan is described in detail in the fund's annual report. If the fund is trading at least 5% above NAV, the fund will reinvest dividends at a discount of 5% to the market price. If the fund is trading at between a 0% and 5% premium, dividends are reinvested at NAV, and if the fund is trading at a discount to NAV, shares are reinvested at market price.

If you hold shares through a broker, you may have to call the broker to request participation in this DRIP, as opposed to the broker's regular reinvestment feature, which would be at market price. For example, in my experience, Schwab (SCHW) requires the client to specifically request the PIMCO DRIP, while Fidelity automatically enrolls its clients in the PIMCO DRIP as long as they select dividend reinvestment in their account online.

With PDO now trading above a 5% premium, investors can get the maximum amount of extra shares by participating in the PIMCO DRIP. For example, starting with 1000 shares and assuming a constant price of $14 and NAV of $13.30, one year of reinvesting at market gets you 1115.3 shares after 1 year (11.5% total return), but one year of reinvesting at NAV gets you 1121.7 shares after one year (12.2% total return).

This is an attractive return even for investors who do not need the income. The drawback is that in a taxable account, investors will need to find some other way to pay the tax on these distributions, most of which are taxed at ordinary income rates. Based on the UNII report, somewhere between 0 and 10% could be classified as return of capital, which lowers cost basis and defers tax until selling. PDO is the third best of the group at distribution coverage over the past 6 months and fiscal year to date; however, other funds have improved their coverage in recent months.

The DRIP makes PDO an attractive choice even for investors who do not need the current income.

Risks

A market panic like the April 2025 tariff tantrum could cause the share price to drop significantly even if the NAV is not seriously impacted. An increase in short-term rates would increase the cost of leverage and reduce distribution coverage, leading to more return of capital or, in severe cases, a dividend cut. A recession would increase the risk of default probability, widening the spreads of most bonds, especially high yield and MBS, which make up a significant portion of PDO. This would reduce their mark-to-market price and impact NAV.

Conclusion

PDO had an excellent 2025, earning equity-like returns thanks to its high leverage as the cost of leverage decreased. A couple of Fed Funds rate cuts this year should continue that trend. The fund also benefitted from narrowing spreads of MBS (coming down from unusually wide levels) and high-yield bonds (staying at unusually narrow levels). The outlook for 2026 is for continued but slower narrowing of MBS spreads and low default rates for leveraged loans and high-yield bonds, which should keep spreads narrow.

In this healthy environment for fixed income, PDO could once again earn a double-digit total return with some capital gains supplementing the nearly 11% distribution yield. That earns PDO an upgrade to Buy. Investors who don't need that much current income can enhance this total return by enrolling in the PIMCO DRIP to reinvest distributions at up to a 5% discount.

data.title: When AI Stops Being 'Tech' And Starts Being The Economy - Investment Themes For 2026 Part 1
data.description:
Before I tell you what I saw in my crystal ball for 2026, keep this line in the front of your mind:

“... the big trends mostly right will not always lead to the outcome that you anticipate.”

- Mackenzie Market Outlook

And here’s the warning label I want on this entire post: adjusting your portfolio is healthy. Replacing your portfolio is usually a mistake. In other words, tweak - don’t flip the table.

The key remains the same: stay invested. Now let’s look at the first theme that matters most for 2026 - the one that’s quietly pulling the whole economy into its orbit.

The big themes that will shape 2026

A word of caution about trends

After reading about AI, nuclear, copper, and grids, you might be tempted to go all-in on the “obvious winners.” The problem is that “need” doesn’t always equal “profit.”

We’ve seen this movie before. A theme becomes real, money floods in, valuations stretch, and suddenly there are more losers than winners - especially for investors who arrive late and pay any price.

The 2026 mindset I want you to adopt is simple:

  • Stop chasing the shiny front layer
  • Start focusing on the companies that enable the trend
  • Prefer businesses that were already strong before the trend became fashionable

That’s how you benefit from the theme without becoming its exit liquidity.

AI isn’t just tech anymore - it’s macro

If 2024-2025 was the market “discovering” AI, 2026 is the market realizing something bigger:

AI is now macro

It drives capital spending, energy demand, grid strain, industrial bottlenecks, market concentration, and (yes) valuations. BlackRock’s 2026 outlook basically treats AI capex as a key driver of growth rather than a sector story.

AI buildouts require:

  • New data centers
  • Transmission lines and grid upgrades
  • Copper, aluminum, steel
  • Electricity (a lot more than people were planning for)
  • Financing (massive amounts of it)

That’s why the opportunity set is broader than “buy a tech darling and hope.” This is moving money into industrials, utilities, materials, energy, and finance - the plumbing behind the dream.

And here’s the part most people miss: this looks and feels like a capex bull market. BlackRock explicitly references multi-trillion-dollar AI capex intentions over the second half of this decade.

“Is it a bubble?”

This is a rare moment when valuation matters. Two groups drove the dot-com bubble:

  1. Pets.com and other empty “Dot-Com” businesses showing clicks and page views, but nothing else.
  2. Established tech giants that spent billions on the internet infrastructure.

We can see both today - cash-burning AI names on one side, and profitable tech leaders investing heavily on the other.

The difference is valuation. In 2000, multiples were extreme (Cisco (CSCO) above 200x earnings; Microsoft (MSFT) and Corning (GLW) above 65x).

Today’s AI leaders are richly valued, but generally nowhere near dot-com extremes (with a few stretched cases like AMD on the chart), and many sit closer to ~30x forward earnings.

So yes, bubble risk exists - mostly if hype and spending run too far - but there’s still room for growth into 2026. That’s why I stick with quality businesses that were strong before AI, using AI as an extra growth tailwind rather than the only thing holding up the dividend triangle.

If you’re reading this and thinking, “Okay, I get the theme... but what do I do with it?” you’re asking the right question.

Before We Get to Energy…

The big takeaway is that AI isn’t just a tech story anymore. It’s a macro force that’s driving a capex cycle across the economy - and it’s already reshaping what wins and what struggles.

But this is where things get interesting. When the economy starts spending at this scale, it creates bottlenecks. And the first bottleneck is obvious: energy.

In Part 2, I’ll walk through the “real-world” themes that AI is accelerating - power and grids, infrastructure and reshoring, the rise of private capital, and even the return of gold as a serious macro trade - without turning your portfolio into a trend-chasing machine.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams: Don't Paint Over This Excellent Opportunity
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

When was the last time you painted a room in your house?

A better question may be, do you have a room in your house that needs to be painted, and you just haven't painted it yet?

According to Better Homes and Gardens, the exterior of your house should be painted about once every 5 years. Possibly more, depending on sun damage or other weather impacts on your home. The inside of your house, however, likely gets more attention for painting, but actually needs it to be painted less often. They recommend waiting no more than 5-15 years to paint each interior room.

For many of us, we like to touch up rooms or paint rooms a different color more frequently than what's considered to be the "maximum" we should wait, because it can refresh or provide a facelift to a home, especially in the face of needing to sell it. To maximize value, a freshly painted home can sell for significantly more than a home with outdated paint by 2%-5% of the house's overall value.

I view paint similarly to how I view cleaning chemicals. People will always be painting some part of their house or some part of their property, just like they will always be cleaning some part of their home or property. While you may not consider paint to be an essential commodity in today's society, it quietly is a societal must that paint be prevalent and available, especially for those who enjoy refreshing their home. Today, I wanted to look at a way to unlock dividends and value from paint.

Strap on your painter's clothes and dive in!

A Dividend Aristocrat With Pricing Power

The general investment thesis for The Sherwin-Williams Company (SHW) is reflected by some simple facts.

The company is the largest player in the global coatings industry, operating over 5,400 stores throughout the U.S., Canada, the Caribbean, and Latin America. Why does this matter? Well, this provides the company with an unmatched "last-mile" distribution network. The sheer number of stores means that there's a high probability that professional contractors are just miles away from an SHW store. That immediate inventory availability and physical presence are a major competitive advantage for the company.

Another strength of SHW's business model is the fact that labor accounts for the vast majority of project costs for a professional contractor. This is why most of SHW's pro contractor customers are willing to tolerate steady price increases from the company. It's usually not worth switching to pinch a few pennies.

SHW put up decent results in Q3 2025. Its total net sales grew by 3.2% year-over-year to $6.36 billion during the quarter. That beat Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus for the quarter by $160 million. SHW's adjusted diluted EPS rose by 6.5% over the year-ago period to $3.59 in the quarter. This topped the consensus by $0.15 during the quarter.

The rate cut cycle playing out should be a growth driver for SHW. As mortgage rates eventually work their way down into the 5% range, demand for its products should eventually improve. This is because home sales should strengthen under such conditions. Its ongoing restructuring actions (i.e., a temporary pause of 401(k) matching contributions) can also help profit margins to gradually expand.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst adjusted diluted EPS consensus anticipates high single-digit to low double-digit percentage growth for 2026 through 2028. That would be off an anticipated 2025 base of $11.37.

SHW's balance sheet is investment-grade, too. The company sports a BBB S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

SHW's 0.9% dividend yield is modest, but its dividend growth rate has been solid. In the last five years, the company has grown its payout by 12.1% annually. This is enough for a Chowder number of 13, which I believe can be largely maintained moving forward. This is because SHW's payout ratio is set to be in the mid- to high-20% range for 2026. That gives it room to extend its 47-year dividend growth streak.

SHW doesn't stand out as an outright bargain. In my view, this fits more in the wonderful business at a fair price category. From the current $340 share price, SHW stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 27.3 (98% weight for 2026 and 2% weight for 2027). This is modestly less than the seven-year average P/E ratio of 28.7.

Moving forward, I think that the seven-year average P/E ratio is a rational, fair value estimate for SHW. The company's annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus of 10.1% is in line with the seven-year CAGR of 12.3%. This points to a company with intact growth potential trading at a 5% discount to fair value ($357 fair value per share estimate).

If SHW matches growth forecasts and returns to fair value, it could generate a more modest 6% total return in 2026. By the end of 2030, the stock could put up approximately 11% annual total returns.

Key risks to watch with SHW include a rapid rise in raw material costs and a sustained housing market downturn.

The most important raw material for the paint industry is arguably titanium dioxide. This comprises a meaningful portion of SHW's cost of goods sold. A pronounced and prolonged spike in spot prices for the raw material could pressure margins if it can't pass higher costs onto customers without pushback.

Another risk to SHW is the role that the housing market plays in its results. On the one hand, high interest rates are keeping existing homeowners in place. This drives demand for renovations via residential repaints. On the other hand, it pressures existing home sales.

The most recent data from Dodge Construction Network's Economics team showed a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.4 million privately owned housing starts in November. That was up 1.7% over the September estimate and 8.7% over the November 2024 estimate. This should lead to strong demand for SHW in the coming months on that front, with the caveat that any slowdown in housing starts could weigh on growth.

Conclusion

SHW is a wonderful company at a fair value. Looking forward, we expect economic conditions and the Trump administration's efforts to drive housing affordability to boost both top and bottom line earnings for SHW. This means SHW is an opportunity for reliable returns and growing dividends that shouldn't be painted over.

Dividend investing isn't just for retirees. Dividend investing is an essential form of investing that can benefit retirees and young investors alike. The longer you can hold a dividend-growing company, the greater the dividend will be compared to what you paid for it. You may spend 40 years or more working towards retirement, but you're also going to spend, hopefully, 30-plus years in retirement, meaning that you don't need to radically alter how you invest based on what age you are, especially when you're buying high-quality dividend growth companies that provide outsized returns.

Retirement can be a stressful time all by itself, entering a new paradigm of life where you're no longer punching the clock Monday through Friday for a paycheck from an employer. You don't need to add the stress of completely changing how you invest in the market to that equation. Dividend investing can provide you with a steady paycheck, a growing paycheck, and high levels of capital preservation and growth. This is the kind of portfolio that we encourage every single investor to heavily consider creating within Dividend Kings. Remove the chaos from your finances and embrace clarity.

data.title: Celestica Q4 Preview: Addressing Google Cloud Rumors (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica (CLS) is set to deliver its Q4 CY25 earnings results next Thursday, January 29, before markets open.

This Canadian AI networking company usually stays out of the spotlight, focused on delivering networking and connectivity solutions to its hyperscaler customers. The reality is that Celestica has been in absolute beast mode, with revenues expected to grow 27% this year and accelerate 31% next year. The company's hyperscaler customers appear to be satisfied with the company allocating an increasing amount of capex dollars towards Celestica's switches and server assembly solutions.

That is until this report that was published yesterday speculating a top hyperscaler client may be moving its server assembly orders elsewhere.

I expect Celestica to confidently address these rumors about its top hyperscaler client next week and believe the company is well positioned to benefit from another year of strong hyperscaler capex deployments.

I am upgrading Celestica's shares to a bullish view ahead of the Q4 ER next week.

Restore Investor Confidence

It has been a while since I covered Celestica on this platform. In June last year, I downgraded Celestica's shares to a neutral view after I told investors that I would be trimming a small portion of my shares. Celestica's shares had aggressively appreciated since last year's April bottom, and that pushed me to trim some of my shares at the time.

But Celestica's shares have surged 2x since my coverage last June, as its top hyperscaler customers continued to pour their respective capex dollars into Celestica's HPC switching and server assembly solutions. The company's performance has obviously superseded my neutral view that I issued last year, and that's purely because of the marvel in how well run this company is.

Here's a chart that I made to frame management's initial projections for the following year's revenue growth guide versus the actual revenue growth that Celestica ended up demonstrating. Note that CY25 & CY26 revenue growth rates are based on management's projections announced in the Q3 ER and 2025 Investor Day, respectively.

As can be seen above, there usually is a wide gap between what Celestica's management projects in the first guide for the following year versus the actual revenue growth that Celestica eventually ends up delivering. As the year progresses, management usually keeps raising their projections, which I have not captured in this chart, but the delta between the initial guide and actual revenue growth signals the strength in Celestica's networking business, which so far has shown no signs of stopping.

I'll frame the perspective in the paragraph above in a different way. Revenue growth has only accelerated every year, with the company well on track to deliver $12.2B in CY25 revenue, per management's projections, implying 27% growth, accelerating from CY24's revenue growth of 21%. That kind of revenue growth and that kind of revenue acceleration has resulted in Celestica winning meaningful market share in the data center switch market. This just shows how well positioned Celestica is in serving demand for its data center switch business, the strength of the company's relationships with its key hyperscaler customers, and the robustness of the company's products and solutions. That's also reflected in the company's product inventory levels, where the company is holding inventory for just 65 days-that's how quickly Celestica is turning its inventory into product revenue.

In the 2025 Investor Day, Celestica's management raised their CY26 projections well above expectations. CY26 revenue projections eventually landed at ~$16B, well above prior expectations of $13.6B. Operating margin guidance has also been beefed up by 40 bp to 7.8% from 7.4% in CY25.

Why does all this context framing matter for Celestica's investors?

Because of yesterday's report that speculated that Alphabet's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Google Cloud, one of Celestica's largest hyperscaler customers, was looking at moving some of its server assembly business away from Celestica, possibly towards the Taiwanese ODM company Inventec. Firstly, I am apprehensive about placing trust in the report purely due to the timing of the report before Celestica's ER next week. Secondly, the report does not fully state whether it is an existing batch of purchase orders that are being moved over to Inventec or if it is a portion of Google Cloud's 2026-27 purchase orders for TPU-based server assembly that is being shared between Celestica, Inventec, or other vendors.

This isn't the first time this kind of speculation has surfaced. In Q2 last year, Celestica's management had to correct an analyst who speculated that the company may have "lost a little bit" of server market share last year. In that call, management reiterated that, in reality, the company was "gaining share with our [their] largest customers" and that the company would benefit from "significant momentum" as these server programs would begin ramping up from Q3 25. In the Q3 call, management reiterated the strength in the business, supported by a sustained ramp in program wins, which I believe gave Celestica's management enough conviction to not only raise revenue growth targets, implying 31% growth this year, but also expect strong growth in free cash to ~$500M.

Therefore, I fully expect management to reiterate their strength in the switching and server business while hashing out details around program wins regarding Google Cloud in the wake of this speculative report pertaining to possible server market share loss.

Outside of the clarifications that I expect management to provide regarding Google Cloud-related customer backlog, I am particularly interested in understanding the strength of their 1.6 Tb networking program solutions. The networking industry should see growth in 800G networking solutions mature while 1.6Tb solutions start to ramp up, representing the next phase of networking growth. Plus, Ethernet is enjoying the best phase of its revolutionary moment in the post-ChatGPT era, and scale-up networks are at a critical juncture of transitioning over towards Ethernet adoption in scale-up. These two developments represent robust target opportunities for Celestica, and I'm very curious about just how well positioned the company is.

Valuations Might Be Slightly Rich

Heading into earnings, investors might view Celestica's valuation multiples as marginal/moderately rich. The company's shares trade at 52x CY25 earnings or 35x CY26 earnings.

Focus on CY25's earnings that are expected to now grow 53% to $5.93 versus 24% at the start of last year. Part of the reason was because Celestica was able to revise up its revenue growth rates through CY25 as demand for its networking solutions. The other reason for superior earnings growth rates was due to strong margin expansion that led to significant earnings revisions leading to the 53% growth in EPS that Celestica is now expecting in CY25.

I believe this combination of strong demand and superior execution by management will lead to another strong year for Celestica, which currently trades at 35x CY26 earnings and is expected to grow ~40% to $8.2 per share. In my view, the current CY26 earnings multiple reflects appropriate entry points for investors once again after the company's valuation multiple looked foamy in Q4 last year.

Risks & What To Know Ahead Of Q4 ER

The big risk here is if Celestica's management actually ends up acknowledging the speculative nature of the report regarding Google Cloud's server assembly program. I believe this could be tied to Google attempting to significantly increase its own DC capacity. At a recent all-hands meeting, Google's execs revealed that they plan to "double AI serving capacity every 6 months." Google may be roping in additional vendors too to serve the search giant's aggressive timeline of adding DC capacity, in my view.

At a macro level, the larger risk is if AI demand starts to slow, which would push hyperscalers to push out their capex deployment timelines, impacting vendors like Celestica.

What I will be watching in next week's ER is if management raises its CY26 projections. Current projections are:

  • CY26 revenues of $16B, imputing to 31% top-line growth.
  • CY26 operating margins are expanding by 40 bp to 7.8%.
  • CY26 adj. EPS of $8.2 per share, imputing to ~40% EPS growth.
  • Free cash of ~$500M

Additionally, I would be looking forward to clarifications about Celestica's server program status with Google Cloud, whether they were able to win more programs or newer client programs.

One last note for investors: insider selling activity appears to be very positive for investors with minimal activity. This is another positive indicator for the strong times ahead.

Takeaway

I believe Celestica continues to enjoy robust AI tailwinds, with its key hyperscaler customer continuing to show faith in the company's networking and server assembly solutions.

Regardless of speculative reports, Celestica will continue to be a beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout race, and I am upgrading my view on Celestica to bullish for now.

data.title: Why I'm Betting The Majority Of My Portfolio On A Cyclical Boom
data.description:
Introduction

When I say that I run a somewhat unorthodox portfolio, I doubt it comes as a surprise to many readers, as most of you are fully aware that I run a very concentrated portfolio of just 16 individual companies, where the biggest five have a weighting of roughly 50%.

Here's what that portfolio looked like as of last weekend:

I'm not going to do a full breakdown of my positions, which is something I will do in the not-too-distant future. I'm bringing this up because slightly more than 60% of my portfolio is invested in stocks that tend to follow what I like to call the economic cycle. In other words, these are companies that tend to perform much better when investors get a signal of broadening and strengthening cyclical growth.

This includes my two biggest energy holdings, my investment in homebuilding-focused companies, all three of my transportation stocks, and my Canadian oil and gas producer.

Especially last year, I invested a lot of capital in cyclical value stocks. It wasn't a popular thesis, as cyclical growth was a mess. That's confirmed by indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which has been below the neutral 50 line for multiple years.

In December, the index took another hit, as we can see below.

This is also one of the reasons why the Mag-7 stocks did so well. They are mostly anti-cyclic, as manufacturing sentiment and housing demand have little impact on their business models. Moreover, when AI became mainstream, it was easy for the market to make the choice to invest in these stocks instead of struggling cyclical stocks.

That decision made sense.

However, as I am a contrarian when it comes to putting capital to work, I used the past year to accumulate cyclical value stocks. It's the reason I did not outperform the market in 2025. However, I believe it will also be the reason why I have a great shot at beating the market in 2026 and likely beyond.

I am so confident because, for the first time, my thesis is getting strong support from fundamental data. That's what this article is all about. I'll walk you through the numbers, explain why they confirm my thesis, and what this means going forward.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

To Me, This Looks Like A Serious Breakout

I believe that in order to fully understand what is going on here, we need to focus on (leading) indicators that actually matter. After all, there's so much data out there that it's easy to lose track. Personally, I look at leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which I just mentioned in the intro.

However, I also look into other numbers, including leading data from the Empire State (New York) and the Philadelphia Fed manufacturing surveys. These are reported ahead of the ISM index. And while they are more volatile and only cover a part of the U.S., when combined, they are a powerful leading indicator that I have been using for more than a decade.

In recent years, both have given off mixed signals. While both were weak (just like the ISM Index), they often gave false recovery signals, as we had many instances where one of them surged while the other declined. It basically told us that the cycle is bottoming, but not at all strengthening. Last year, that was the correct call.

Now, we're seeing more synchronized growth, as the Philly Fed General Activity Index surged to 12.6 points, which is its highest level since September. The Empire State General Business Conditions Index rose to 7.7 points after a dip in December.

Here's what the Empire State numbers look like:

This is the Philly Fed chart:

When we combine them, we see that the industrial economy is moving from contraction into expansion. If this persists, we can officially make the case that the economy is going from a "soft landing" scenario to a "no landing" scenario, which means the U.S. is likely to avoid a recession altogether.

This is important, as I have often made the case that we're in for higher inflation, but not a recession. My issue with the stagflation thesis that many had was that the "flation" part was right, but the "stag" part isn't confirmed. The opposite seems to be happening.

Needless to say, you could make the case that this is just another fake upswing signal. After all, we had many over the past 12-14 months. That would be a great comment and something we need to address to give my thesis a strong foundation.

I believe this upswing is for real, as we have a number of tailwinds hitting the "real" economy.

  • AI hyperscalers are "plowing" hundreds of billions into the economy this year by building data centers. This capital is expected to accelerate going forward (see below) and end up in construction, energy, and everything related to data centers, power generation, and the grid. I like to call this QE-like data center spending.

  • Deregulation is occurring. This includes the One Big Beautiful Bill. Apollo showed that this could boost GDP growth by nearly 0.1% this year, supported by tailwinds like immediate capital expense decisions and other factors.

  • Although the uncertainty surrounding Greenland has caused trade war fears to make a comeback, in general, the situation has become more predictable between nations like the US and China.
  • I believe the U.S. government and the Fed are eager to "run it hot," meaning they somewhat ease the fight against inflation in order to broaden economic growth. Initiatives like $200 billion to buy mortgages are one way to improve liquidity and ease the burden on the housing market. I expect to see much more fiscal and monetary support this year.

With that said, I thought about this a lot, as I believe we are seeing a "bullwhip" effect. That's why I like cyclical companies like transportation company Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL). I am bringing this company up quite frequently, as it is a key holding of mine and a terrific example of my thesis.

Transportation companies have been through a transportation recession in recent years. One major issue was post-pandemic inventory destocking. Now, this is signaling a very bullish turnaround. For example, in January, the Philly Fed report saw orders jump to 14.4 points. However, the inventory index declined to -8.4. The Empire State report saw new orders surge to 6.6 points, while inventories remained in contraction (at -2.1 points).

Essentially, this is the "sweet spot" where you want to be as an investor. If both orders and inventories were rising, it could suggest a late-cycle development. However, we have both rising orders and falling supply, which is creating an imbalance that requires a lot more production.

To me, this seems like clear early-stage developments.

On a side note, as we head into the hot phase of the earnings season, I'll be on the lookout for company comments and numbers to add more color to this thesis.

Moreover, both of these indices saw strong pricing, which confirms my inflation thesis, and weak employment, which confirms the AI thesis, where companies are not eager to boost employment levels due to a bigger focus on margin optimization.

While this may be a bad sign for the labor market, it is not a signal of weakening growth, as the "AI economy" is different. Now, companies are in a better spot to grow without having to rely on labor to do so. Personally, I don't like that, yet my opinion doesn't matter.

What matters is that we're now looking at a high-probability upswing in leading indicators and what could be the first sustainable cyclical upswing since the pandemic.

If that happens (I am convinced that it will happen), this has major implications for the market and my investments.

Why All Of This Matters So Much For The Market

I don't just discuss these things because they are critical to my portfolio.

No, this is important for the entire market.

For starters, the chart below compares the year-over-year performance of the S&P 500 (blue line) to the ISM Manufacturing Index. While the market is very tech-heavy, it still needs long-term support from cyclical growth.

In light of the market's elevated valuation, a rebound could make the recent rally a lot more sustainable.

As you can imagine, if the market, in general, benefits, there are areas that benefit more.

That's the cyclical value space I spent so many hours on last year.

Think about transportation companies, machinery producers, diversified suppliers, larger conglomerates, housing companies, distributors, energy companies, and so many more.

Again, that covers more than 60% of my portfolio. The remaining 40% consists of less cyclical aerospace companies, oil and gas midstream companies that do not rely on commodity prices, and stock exchanges. The majority of my portfolio will likely get a substantial tailwind if my thesis is right.

The chart below shows the ratio between industrial stocks (XLI) and the S&P 500 (black line) compared to the ISM Manufacturing Index. At the end of this year, I expect both the black and the red lines to be much higher, which would indicate that industrial stocks have outperformed the S&P 500.

Next up is the chart showing the year-over-year performance of transportation stocks (IYT) compared to the ISM Index. Historically speaking, an ISM surge to the high-50 range (or higher) results in 30-40% annual returns for transportation companies.

These are places with alpha during upswings and elevated volatility and downtrends when economic growth goes south. That's why I focus on the highest-quality players that I buy during downswings and keep when things go well.

Ideally, I hold these stocks for decades and only buy them when there's "blood in the streets." Last year, that was the case. Now, I'm still buying, as we're in the early innings of what I expect to become a steep upswing. Once that materializes, I will look for other opportunities.

For now, however, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

For the first time, my contrarian thesis on cyclical value is getting strong fundamental support, as it looks like a synchronized breakout in some of my favorite leading indicators.

Especially the developments in new orders and inventories cause me to believe we are in the early innings of a rebound. When adding policy support and aggressive data center spending on top of other themes like economic re-shoring, I am a big fan of cyclical value and believe that's where the alpha will be for the foreseeable future.

I'm so sure of it that I have invested most of my capital in this space over the past two years. Now, I have more than 60% of my portfolio tied to this thesis. If I'm even somewhat right, I expect to see massive tailwinds.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is me being wrong. If we don't get a recovery, the economy could remain stuck and mostly dependent on AI spending. It would cause recession odds to spike and likely end up with me lagging the market a bit.

Other risks are a new trade war escalation, an inflation rebound so bad that it forces the Fed to hike rates, or even a full-blown stagflation. Triggers could be a steep increase in energy prices. It could result in rates pressuring housing, general credit, and other factors that hurt the consumer and cause inventory restocking to stall.

I do not expect that to happen, but for my strategy, it's the biggest downside risk.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: Buy This Dividend King On Sale Now
data.description:
This article was written by Kody Kester (Kody's Dividends).

As a long-term investor, I believe it's important to take the long view and remain calm amid panic when a company's fundamentals are sound. This is what makes earnings season so fascinating to me.

You never know whose earnings report is going to spark panic among "investors" and finicky trading algorithms. It's also difficult to predict what will freak the market out on a stock.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT) will probably go down as one of the bigger post-earnings slumps of this earnings season. Today, it slumped 10% with the release of its fourth-quarter earnings. Q1 2026 midpoint adjusted EPS guidance of $1.15 ($1.1 to $1.18) was just below the consensus of $1.20. For the full year 2026, though, guidance was largely on point.

When I last covered ABT with a Buy rating in January 2025, I thought it could realistically deliver double-digit percentage annual adjusted diluted EPS over the next few years. The company's AA-rated balance sheet was another positive. Lastly, shares were slightly discounted.

One calendar year later, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating. Despite the noise of weakness from the Nutrition and Diagnostics segments, ABT's core products (FreeStyle Libre, the Volt PFA System, etc.) continued to perform well. The company's pending acquisition of Exact Sciences is another reason for optimism. ABT's interest coverage ratio points to a financially sound business. Clinching my buy case is the fact that shares are now deeply undervalued.

Abbott is Revving Up the Growth Engine

As I alluded to earlier, earlier today, ABT shared its financial results for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company's reported net sales grew by 3.8% year-over-year to $11.46 billion during the quarter. This came up $330 million shy of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter.

Factoring in foreign currency translation tailwinds to the tune of 1.4%, ABT's organic net sales increased by 3% over the year-ago period for the fourth quarter. On the other side of the equation, COVID-19 testing net sales hurt the company's net sales to the tune of 0.8% during the quarter. Overall, organic net sales excluding COVID-19 tests edged 3.8% higher year-over-year in the quarter.

So, what elements contributed to ABT's topline miss for the fourth quarter? The Nutrition segment was the leading cause behind the company's net sales miss during the quarter. Its reported net sales declined 8.9% (-9.1% organically) over the year-ago period to $1.94 billion in the quarter. As Chairman and CEO Robert Ford put it in his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, this was driven by volume challenges. Raising prices in recent years to keep up with surging commodity costs has suppressed consumer demand as consumers have become more price sensitive. These issues were most pronounced in the Pediatric business, with U.S. net sales tailing off 17.7% to $463 million.

The secondary pressure point for ABT for the fourth quarter came from its Diagnostics segment. The segment's reported net sales decreased by 2.5% year-over-year (-3.6% organically) to $2.46 billion during the quarter. COVID-19 testing-related sales were nearly cut in half to $89 million in the quarter, which is to be expected as the final remnants of the COVID-19 testing revenue wash out over time. Backing out COVID-19 testing headwinds, the segment's net sales were relatively flat, down 0.2%.

On the flip side, ABT displayed tremendous strength in its Medical Devices and Established Pharmaceuticals segments for the fourth quarter. The former's net sales jumped 12.3% (+10.4% organically) over the year-ago period to $5.68 billion during the quarter. Momentum from top products like the FreeStyle Libre continuous glucose monitor franchise and the Aveir leadless pacemaker was to credit for this growth in the quarter. As was the new product launch of the Volt PFA System in the U.S. (ABT's first PFA offering in the U.S.).

The Established Pharmaceuticals segment reported a 9.0% surge (+7.0% organically) in net sales to $1.38 billion for the fourth quarter. That was made possible by double-digit growth in India and several countries across Latin America and the Middle East.

ABT's adjusted diluted EPS vaulted 11.9% higher year-over-year to $1.50 during the fourth quarter. This came in $0.01 above the analyst consensus in the quarter.

For full-year 2026, ABT anticipates midpoint organic net sales growth of 7% (6.5% to 7.5%). The company's midpoint adjusted diluted EPS guidance of $5.675 would represent 10% growth.

As FreeStyle Libre CGMs continue to increase their penetration, this is one growth catalyst for ABT. Another growth driver for the company is the recent launch of the Volt PFA System in the U.S. Currently, there are around 12 million people in the U.S. with AFib. Over the next 20 years, this is expected to double. The combination of this being a minimally invasive procedure with high efficacy should sustain strong growth for this product as well.

ABT also recognizes that it's going to have to play the volume game with its Nutrition segment if it hopes to revitalize growth there. In the fourth quarter, the company began implementing price and promotion initiatives to kickstart that process. In the meantime, the company expects to launch at least eight new products over the next 12 months to breathe new life into the segment.

Finally, ABT announced a couple of months ago that it was acquiring Exact Sciences (EXAS) in a $21 billion deal. This will add the market-leading noninvasive colorectal cancer screening option (Cologuard), the personalized treatment decision technology (Oncotype), and the multi-cancer early detection blood test (Cancerguard) products to its portfolio. The deal is expected to close in Q2 2026 and would add complementary, high-growth products to its portfolio.

These factors have me cautiously optimistic that ABT can deliver 10.5% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth through 2028, off a 2025 base of $5.15.

ABT is also financially stable. The company's interest coverage ratio in 2025 was 43.5. This supports an AA- S&P credit rating with a negative outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to ABT's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and ABT's Q4 2025 Infographic).

Fair Value is Approaching $135 a Share

In the last 12 months, ABT's shares have returned -7%. That's a stark contrast to the 15% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time. This happened as ABT's adjusted diluted EPS has grown, and I'm now incorporating 2026 and 2027 forecasts into my forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input. That has taken shares from a slight discount a year ago to a much more pronounced undervaluation today.

At the current $108 share price (as of January 22nd, 2026), ABT is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19. This is well below the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/E ratio (and my fair value estimate) of 23.6.

Why do I still believe that ABT deserves such a valuation multiple? Well, the company's 10.5% annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is well above the 10-year CAGR of 7.4%. This suggests ABT's growth profile is intact.

Currently, the calendar year 2026 is 6% in the books. That leaves another 94% of 2026 and 6% of 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $5.71.

Plugging that into my fair value P/E ratio of 23.6, I compute a fair value of $134 a share. Relative to the current share price, that equates to a 20% discount to fair value.

If ABT reverts to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could have a 13% upside by the end of 2026 (not including the 10% drop today). Through 2030, ABT could generate 12% annual total returns (also not accounting for the selloff today).

The Payout Can Keep Climbing

ABT's 2.3% forward dividend yield comes in meaningfully above the healthcare sector's 1.3% median forward yield. This represents a 60 basis point widening of the yield spread versus last January. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System issues B and C+ grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (the former of which is up from a B- grade last January). These grades also don't yet consider the selloff today, which could incrementally improve them.

ABT is just as much of an income growth story as it is a starting income story, too. The company has grown its payout for 54 consecutive years (though it shows up as 12 years in the Quant System), which is considerably more established than the sector median dividend growth streak of 3 years. This explains the A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from last January).

ABT's dividend is highly sustainable as well, which sets it up to build on this dividend growth streak for the foreseeable future. The company's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the mid-40% range for 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, that would be comfortably better than the 60% payout ratio that rating agencies desire from the industry. In terms of free cash flow, ABT's dividend is about as well-covered. In 2026, the FCF payout ratio is anticipated to be in the high-40% range.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the risk of a payout reduction over the next 12 months at 0.3%. In the next average recession, this rises to just 1.8%. Unsurprisingly, the Quant System awards a B+ grade for overall dividend safety (up from a B- grade from last January).

Looking ahead, the Quant System forecasts a 6.2% annual forward dividend per share growth rate for ABT (the same as it was last January). That would be better than the sector median of 4.8%. And given the company's reputation for high single-digit annual dividend growth, this is arguably a bit conservative. I could foresee dividend growth being closer to its 3-year CAGR of 7.7% in the years ahead. At any rate, ABT earns an A grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (unchanged from last January).

Risks to Consider

ABT arguably has a bright future ahead. Still, some risks could materially impact the investment thesis.

The first notable risk that ABT faces is its pending acquisition of Exact Sciences. This deal could potentially face antitrust challenges. If that does happen, significant divestitures could be required to get the deal across the finish line. What's more, there is execution risk if this acquisition is ultimately finalized. If ABT can't scale Exact's direct-to-consumer model and navigate the changing reimbursement landscape for multi-cancer early detection tests, its success could be diminished.

Another risk to the company is that the majority of its net sales in 2025 (61.4%) were derived outside the United States (ABT's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release). Unfavorable foreign currency translation could pressure its financial results in the future. More importantly, ABT must comply with a wide range of regulations from one market to the next. If it fails to do so, it could be hit by regulators with sizable penalties.

One final risk facing ABT is the overhang of multidistrict litigation (MDL) regarding its cow's-milk-based preterm infant formulas. As of January 2026, there were around 950 total cases filed, with 769 pending in the MDL. The plaintiffs allege that ABT failed to warn them that its cow's milk products could raise the risk of low birth weight infants (preemies) developing necrotizing enterocolitis by anywhere from six to 20 times versus preemies who were fed breast milk.

Thus far, ABT has won the first three bellwether federal cases via summary judgment for insufficient evidence that cow's milk formula was the specific cause of NEC in impacted infants. Still, the company has more cases against which it must defend itself. The first is likely to get underway this August.

In state-level courts that are sometimes viewed as more plaintiff-friendly, ABT may not fare as well. If it can't get such verdicts overturned, this could potentially cost it billions of dollars in legal damages.

Summary: The Market Selloff is a Gift

It's not every day that a Dividend King sells off 9% following earnings. In my view, this represents a clear buying opportunity. ABT has the catalysts to deliver double-digit percentage adjusted diluted EPS growth. The balance sheet is healthy, too.

And while ABT does face integration risk with its Exact Sciences acquisition and pending litigation, that's arguably priced into the valuation. This provides a realistic path to 12%+ annual total returns over the next five years. That's why I'm standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: When Quality Goes On Sale: 2 Dividend Growth Stocks To Consider For Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

One of my biggest lessons in investing, I would say, is my lack of patience. While I've gotten better, this is still something I often struggle with today.

I say that to say that if you're patient enough, the market will always present some compelling investment opportunities for those who are patient. And these contrarian opportunities can lead to potential outperformance.

In reference to these two stocks I discuss today, both have been beaten down in the past year. Whether it's from tariffs, higher-for-longer interest rates impacting consumers, or even overvaluation.

As a result, both currently trade well below their five-year averages, a potentially compelling buying investment opportunity for long-term investors. And this is despite lingering risks from inflation, economic uncertainty, and changing consumer behavior.

In this article, I discuss why these two stocks are underperforming, their fundamentals, and why long-term dividend growth investors should consider them for their portfolio.

T-Mobile (TMUS)

T-Mobile is a stock that has underperformed in the past year. As you can see in the chart below, the stock has significantly underperformed the market in the past year, down close to 16%. The S&P 500 (SP500) is up close to 17% in comparison.

In my opinion, I believe this has less to do with T-Mobile's fundamentals and more to do with them being overvalued. I discussed this in an article back in July. As a result, I downgraded the stock from a buy to a hold.

At the time, TMUS was trading around $240 a share and up 39% at the time of writing. Back then, I warned investors that the biggest risk was the valuation and that they should consider adding closer to the $200 level.

Since then, the price has continued to slide, leading me to believe the telecom giant is now in oversold territory. TMUS recently reached a new 52-week low of $184 and currently sits slightly above, around $185 a share.

At the time of writing, T-Mobile's attractive forward P/E of 18x appears to be an attractive entry point using the earnings estimate.

For long-term investors, this should also be viewed as an attractive entry as the current price sits well below its five-year average. Below, analysts are expecting TMUS to see strong growth of 18.66% through 2030.

For a stock expected to see near 20% growth, I think a P/E of 22x - 25x is very reasonable. Besides their upside potential, one of the most attractive metrics about TMUS is the dividend growth since initiating one in late 2023.

While telecom companies are known for solid payout ratios around 60%, TMUS's free cash flow payout ratio in their last quarter was 21.6%. This is compared to 54.4% for peer Verizon (VZ).

TMUS raised their dividend back in September by an impressive 16%. And since initiating one in 2023, the dividend has grown by an impressive 57%, from $0.65 to $1.02.

Despite their short dividend growth track record, the low payout ratio leads me to believe that TMUS will continue to grow the dividend by double digits for at least the next five years.

Moreover, frequent share repurchases will likely enable solid earnings growth, leading to additional dividend increases and upside potential. Using the fair value of 24.42x, TMUS offers strong upside to the price target of roughly $393 a share by the end of 2028.

Investors may view TMUS' debt balance as a problem due to high-interest rates. But the low payout ratio gives plenty of retained cash flow to pay down debt.

Moreover, their leverage ratio of 2.4x is reasonable. For comparison, Verizon had the lowest of the peer group at 2.2x. AT&T (T) had the highest at 2.59x.

Although risks linger from increased competition that could result in a potential loss of subscribers, any further volatility should be viewed as a buying opportunity.

However, I believe the company's frequent buybacks, acquisitions, and lower base rates will provide tailwinds, allowing TMUS to continue to dominate competitors. And I suspect the telecom giant could return to its normal valuation in the next 12 to 24 months.

#2 Otis Worldwide (OTIS)

Otis is another out-of-favor stock at the moment. Like T-Mobile, the elevator giant is also in the red in the past year, down more than 6%. Since declining after Q2 earnings from above $100 a share, the stock seems to have bottomed out around $85 and has since traded sideways.

Otis' problems, in my opinion, have stemmed from the higher-for-longer interest rate environment and tariffs, which have impacted margins.

Since August, OTIS has been trading in a range of $85 to $90 a share, a buying opportunity for long-term investors. Due to margin pressures, the company suffered a 20% decline in its New Equipment segment, led by headwinds in China.

During Q3 earnings, operating margins declined from 6.4% to 4.7% year-over-year. Additionally, the sell-off this past summer was led by a revision in guidance by management in the second quarter.

In the latest quarter, pressures continued to weigh on the company. But cost savings initiatives of $240 million by fiscal year's end are expected to mitigate headwinds. They also raised their EPS outlook in Q3, representing 5%–7% growth for the fiscal year.

Additionally, Otis controls a large part of the market share in the elevator industry at close to 37% at the end of 2023. And despite headwinds likely to continue for at least the near term, Otis remains a compelling investment for patient investors willing to hold for the next three to five years.

Lower base rates should provide tailwinds. Frequent share repurchases, retained cash flow, and a solid balance sheet put the company in a favorable position to successfully navigate current headwinds.

Otis only has $7.6 billion in long-term debt and $840 million in cash available. The free cash flow payout ratio through three quarters was solid at 71.8%. For the fiscal year, free cash flow is expected to be $1.45 billion, a slight increase from $1.437 in 2024.

As a reference point, Otis ended 2024 with a free cash flow payout ratio of 37.5%. This gives them ample room for future dividend increases and buybacks. In Q3, management exhausted their $800 million buyback program.

And long term, this will likely continue to drive earnings growth in the high, single-digit range, in my opinion. For the next four years, earnings are expected to grow at a CAGR of 11.6%.

As a result, I believe this should allow solid dividend growth in the 5%–8% range as well. Like T-Mobile, Otis doesn't have a long dividend track record, initiating a dividend in 2020.

Since then, the dividend has more than doubled from $0.20 to $0.42 a share. Their last increase was 7.7% to $0.42. And as previously mentioned, I foresee mid- to high single-digit increases going forward, making OTIS a solid wealth compounder for long-term investors.

Like TMUS, the elevator giant also offers strong upside with a price target of $141 by the end of 2028. Furthermore, the stock received an upgrade in December due to diminishing China headwinds and trades below its five-year average of 24.66x.

One of Otis' risks still includes tariffs. Recently, President Trump threatened to impose tariffs on several European nations starting next month. And these are expected to increase in June if no deal is reached.

Currently, Otis expects organic sales growth in the mid-single digits. However, if tariffs are indeed imposed, this may impact this, leading to further volatility. But investors should view this as a buying opportunity to dollar-cost average.

Investor Takeaway

T-Mobile and Otis are great long-term dividend growers with strong fundamentals and solid moats.

Long-term investors should view their price underperformances as buying opportunities, as I believe lower base rates should provide tailwinds going forward.

As a result, I expect both could return to their fair value ratios in the next 12 to 24 months.

Despite lingering risks from macro factors that could continue to impact margins, I'm confident both companies can return to double-digit growth in the near to medium future

data.title: Northeast Bank's Surge Doesn't Necessitate A Downgrade
data.description:
I have, over the last few years now, established a pretty solid track record when it comes to banks. One company that I have recently gotten very right is Northeast Bank (NBN). With a market capitalization of $921.3 million as of this writing, it is certainly not the largest bank that I have analyzed. But it is far from the smallest as well. Over the last several months now, I have been bullish about the institution. And that bullishness has paid off nicely. Since I reaffirmed it as a ‘buy’ candidate back in October of last year, the stock has risen 15.3%. That dwarfs the 3.6% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I upgraded the stock to a ‘buy’ in June of last year, shares have achieved an upside of 31.4%. That's very close to double the 16.9% increase that the market enjoyed.

Looking at the picture today, I see a bank that continues to achieve some nice growth. The balance sheet is improving, and that's taking the income statement up with it. Asset quality remains high, though the stock is not as cheap as it once was. The good news is that, even with the share price increase that we have seen, shares are cheap enough to justify my previous bullish assessment. That's why I don't have an issue reaffirming it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate at this time.

It is always possible that my mind could change on the matter as new data comes in. We do know that, around the end of the month, tentatively January 26th, the management team at Northeast Bank will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts seem mixed, with revenue forecasted to rise while profitability drops. But whatever transpires, the devil will be in the details.

Too Early to Downgrade Northeast Bank

For the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, which is the only new quarter for which we have data available, Northeast Bank posted mixed results. For instance, the value of deposits did unfortunately drop, declining from $3.38 billion down to $3.25 billion. But the good news is that all of this decline was driven by brokered deposits, which are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial firms. So even though this decline in the size of the balance sheet is disappointing in and of itself, where that decline came from was as good as it could have been.

As deposits for the institution decline, we did see a small drop in the value of loans. But honestly, it amounts to a rounding error in the grand scheme of things. And I do mean that quite literally. From the end of 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, the value of loans dipped by only about $2 million. That was enough to keep the overall amount at about $3.71 billion. The vast majority of the loan portfolio is tied up in commercial real estate. Management had about $2.79 billion dedicated to that category in the first quarter. A distant second place belongs to commercial and industrial, with $849.6 million. The rest of the portfolio, meanwhile, is comprised of residential real estate and just a modicum of consumer loans.

Other aspects of the balance sheet should not be ignored. It is important to note, however, that the other parts of the balance sheet are quite small by comparison. For instance, the company had only $15.6 million of securities on its books in the first quarter of 2026. That's down from the $22.7 million reported at the end of 2025. Cash and cash equivalents went from $413.6 million down to $341.8 million. And at the same time as this, debt dropped slightly from $320.2 million to $316.8 million.

Normally when you see multiple areas of the balance sheet shrink, you would expect to see revenue and profits worsen. But surprisingly, that's not what transpired. Management actually saw net interest income grow from $38.6 million to $48.6 million. This was in spite of the fact that the net interest margin for the institution declined from 4.90% to 4.59%. This might all seem contradictory. But what's important here is that, even though we have been talking about changes in the balance sheet since the end of 2024, and those have shown a decline in size year over year for each fiscal quarter, for the first quarter, we have seen a pretty big increase in the size of the balance sheet. For instance, total interest-earning assets jumped, on an average basis, 31.9% year over year. That absolutely can impact net interest income in a big and positive way.

Non-interest income also grew, rising from $4.1 million to $4.7 million. This was thanks to an increase in the gain on sales of SBA loans from $3.3 million to $4.1 million. Without that, we would have actually seen a drop in non-interest income. As a result of these improvements, net profits amounted to $22.5 million during the first quarter of 2026. By comparison, for the same time the previous year, profitability amounted to $17.1 million.

It is too early to really rely much on projections for 2026. But if we were to rely too much on 2026 results, based on what we have seen already for the year, we would be looking at a firm that is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.2. This is just a hair above the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. And, in the chart above, you can see that the institution comes in below all but one of the five institutions that I compared it to. So on an absolute basis, that isn't exactly undervalued, while on a relative basis, it's on the cheaper end of the spectrum. In the chart below, I then looked at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In addition to being expensive on an absolute basis, it's also significantly more costly than any of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart.

The reason for this high-priced book and price-to-tangible book multiple situation can be chalked up to high asset quality as well. In the first chart below, you can see that Northeast Bank has a return on assets reading of 2.13%. That's more than double the 1% maximum that I like to see. And it puts the company tied with one other player as the top prospect in that chart. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 17.64%, Northeast Bank is massively superior to any of the five banks that I compared it to. And it is also comfortably above the 10% minimum that I target.

Other than recent fluctuations and the size of the balance sheet, the only real complaint that I have of the institution is that its credit quality is not quite as high as I would like. I typically prefer, for instance, for no more than 0.50% of loans and assets to be classified as non-performing. But as of the end of the most recent quarter, these numbers amounted to 0.90% and 0.84%, respectively. Both of those, however, represented improvements over what was seen a year earlier, when the company notched readings of 1.06% and 0.94%, respectively.

At the start of this article, I made the case that my opinion on the business could change when new data is released. That is just around the corner, with January 26th estimated for that release date. As we approach that time, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $54.8 million. This would actually be up slightly from the $54.4 million reported a year earlier. On the other hand, profitability is expected to decline, dropping from $2.74 per share to $2.45 per share. This would mean a drop in net profits from $22.4 million to $20.7 million. Obviously, we will need to read deeper into that to understand what all is going on if analysts are correct, and even if they aren't. I generally don't like declining profits. But given the overall health of the company, that would likely be more of a bump in the road than anything.

Takeaway

On the whole, I would say that Northeast Bank is most certainly an interesting prospect. It's not exactly the best candidate out there. But it's definitely not the worst either. The stock has performed well in the time that I have been bullish about it. And honestly, at some point, it will make sense to downgrade it. But for now, I believe that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ is the appropriate choice.

data.title: I'm Betting Big On These 8-10% Yields For Early Retirement
data.description:
High-yield stocks are a great way to invest for early retirement because they enable you to accelerate the growth of your passive income stream to fund your living expenses once you stop working. Additionally, the way in which they deliver their returns, namely through passive income from dividends, is generally much more reliable than depending on market performance with an index investing strategy.

While investing in dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) or the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) are simple and popular investment vehicles for retiring on dividends, their yields range between 3% and 4% and are often too low for many investors looking to retire early. Therefore, high-yielding stocks can be a more attractive alternative.

However, the big risk with high-yield stocks is that their high yields are unsustainable, and you end up making deep dividend cuts that not only result in disappointing passive income for investors but also lead to steep share price declines, leaving investors in the worst possible shape. Additionally, even if they do sustain their payouts, they often fail to deliver enough passive income to both meet current living expenses and grow that income to keep pace with inflation over time.

As a result, investors need to be highly selective in what high-yield stocks they pick. With this in view, in this article, I’m going to share two high-yield opportunities that I’m betting big on for early retirement.

8% Yielding Opportunity With Strong Growth Potential

The first opportunity I will discuss is MPLX LP (MPLX). MPLX is a midstream MLP that issues a K-1 tax form. So investors should keep the tax implications of this in mind before investing in it. Of course, you can invest in 1099-issuing ETF alternatives like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), which can still give you a similar distribution yield, strong distribution growth, and exposure to MPLX, along with several other midstream businesses, without having to deal with the K-1 tax form, though you will have to pay a substantial expense ratio of 0.85% and have a less advantageous tax treatment than you would enjoy by simply owning individual MLPs like MPLX.

MPLX is a pick that I feel confident betting big on because it combines a high yield with a healthy balance sheet, a strong business model, and attractive long-term growth prospects. Its balance sheet is strong, as evidenced by its BBB credit rating and its well-diversified, well-laddered debt maturities through 2030, with no more than $2 billion maturing each year and, in some years, even less than $1 billion maturing. Meanwhile, its leverage ratio remains well below its upper limit of four times, which gives it significant flexibility to be able to repurchase units, invest in attractive opportunities that come its way, and continue to grow its distribution at an aggressive clip.

Moreover, its business model is durable and defensive, with limited commodity price exposure thanks to having a highly contracted business model that is diversified across crude oil, product logistics, natural gas, and NGL services. Its crude oil and products logistics business is highly integrated with Marathon Petroleum’s (MPC) value chains, which have a competitive advantage and are competitively positioned within their regions.

Additionally, its natural gas and NGL services handle over 10% of all natural gas produced in the United States. MPLX also has strong growth potential, as it is leaning aggressively into its natural gas and NGL opportunities, with it accelerating its capital expenditures from $0.9 billion in 2022 to $1.2 billion in 2023 to $1.9 billion in 2024 to a whopping $5.5 billion in 2025, with the vast majority of that coming from strategic bolt-on acquisitions. Additionally, over 90% of its 2025 growth capital was spent on natural gas and NGLs, as it is leaning into the powerful growth tailwinds behind those sectors.

These all combine to provide strong support for its attractive distribution that currently yields 8% on a next 12-month basis, and management has guided for double-digit distribution growth for the foreseeable future. Analysts also see this as a likely outcome, as they are forecasting an 11.5% distribution CAGR through 2027.

Putting this all together makes MPLX one of the most compelling combinations of high current yield and inflation-beating growth backed by a durable and defensive business model.

10%+ Yielding Opportunity With a Strong Balance Sheet

The second investment that I’m betting big on for retirement is Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC). While many BDCs (BIZD) - such as FS KKR (FSK) - are seeing their dividends cut or appear highly likely to be cut in the near future, KBDC has been able to support its payout and appears positioned to be able to continue doing so. This is because it currently covers its dividend by 1.08 times with its net investment income, while also having a leverage ratio of just 1.01 times. A leverage ratio at that level gives it significant additional debt capacity that it can put onto its balance sheet to enhance its return on equity and further support its base dividend before it reaches its upper limit of 1.25 times.

Meanwhile, KBDC’s portfolio is in a strong position thanks to having 94% of its investments in first-lien senior secured loans, with its investments diversified across 108 borrowers spread across numerous industries.

Additionally, it has no near-term refinancing headwinds, as its closest maturity is in 2027 and is fixed interest rate debt with a rate of 8.65% that I am quite confident they could refinance today at a materially lower interest rate if it were to mature. The only reason they are not able to refinance it early is that prepayment penalties would be applied to them if they paid it off early and would make it uneconomical to do so. Nevertheless, they should only see tailwinds moving forward on the interest expense side of things, since much of their debt is floating rate, and the small portion that is fixed rate is at higher interest rates that they should be able to refinance at lower interest rates in the coming years.

This gives me even further confidence in their ability to support their dividend moving forward. Not only that, but their 1.01 times leverage ratio is further overstated in terms of their actual earnings power because, during the quarter, they had an average debt-to-equity of 0.97 times.

On top of that, the portfolio health remains quite strong, with only 1.4% of the portfolio, on a fair value basis, on non-accrual. The company is also buying back stock through its share purchase program to take advantage of the current discount to net asset value and help to add leverage to the value, further optimizing returns. These buybacks are accretive on an accretion basis and also help to enhance the dividend coverage ratio.

Additionally, KBDC currently yields 10.8% and trades at a 9.6% discount to its net asset value.
